steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Set up the angle relationships
steps.content: Two intersecting lines create four angles. Vertical angles are equal, and adjacent angles are supplementary (sum to 180°). If one angle is $(9x - 140)$°, then its vertical angle is also $(9x - 140)$°, and each of the other two angles is $180 - (9x - 140) = (320 - 9x)$°.

steps.title: List all achievable sums of two angles
steps.content:
The only distinct sums possible from any two of the four angles are:

(1) Two equal acute angles: $(9x-140)+(9x-140) = (18x-280)$°
(2) Two equal obtuse angles: $(320-9x)+(320-9x) = (-18x+640)$°
(3) One of each (a supplementary pair): $(9x-140)+(320-9x) = 180$°

steps.title: Identify which choice is NOT achievable
steps.content:
• Choice A — $\left(-18x+280\right)^\circ$: Does not equal any of the three achievable sums. Not achievable. ✓
  • Choice B — $\left(-18x+640\right)^\circ$: Equals the sum of the two obtuse angles. Achievable; eliminated. ✗
  • Choice C — $\left(18x-280\right)^\circ$: Equals the sum of the two acute angles. Achievable; eliminated. ✗
  • Choice D — $180^\circ$: Equals the sum of one acute and one obtuse angle. Achievable; eliminated. ✗
That is the correct answer.
